text: A large direct right inguinal hernia sac is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection medially, just lateral to the medial umbilical ligament, progressing from medial to lateral in the surgical field.
The surgeon maintains a thin dissection on the peritoneal lining and transverse fascia laterally, displacing the fat and leaving it on the abdominal wall.
The surgeon dissects far medially to expose Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, revealing these anatomical landmarks during the robotic right direct inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon dissects laterally along the ileopubic tract, positioned above the myopectineal orifice, as part of the robotic right direct inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies a large direct inguinal hernia sac and pushes the anti-sac up and away, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia.
The surgeon uses electrocautery and applies counter traction, causing the tissue to separate and fall away smoothly.
The surgeon navigates around the spermatic cord and vas deferens, revealing these structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small femoral hernia plug in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue off the right iliac region, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon pushes the vas deferens away while maneuvering around the corner, maintaining clear visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies a very small cord lipoma adjacent to the spermatic cord.
The surgeon dissects tissue all the way to the pubic tubercle, approaching the symphysis pubis medially.
The surgeon positions the mesh medially, ensuring it extends beneath the pubic tubercle and remains sufficiently medial to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon performs a high medial ligation by suturing the hernia sac to the medial aspect of the hernia defect, followed by suturing to Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle.
The surgeon performs a high lateral fixation of the mesh, securing it in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon closes the flap using a one-handed running suture technique with PDS (polydioxanone) suture material.
The surgeon sutures the hernia defect back onto the flap closure site, ensuring the defect is securely closed to reduce the chance of recurrence.
